target: Per-WIN post_hoc audit. Proposes standardized closing sentences (one per category T/D/V/O) to be appended to the detail_verdict_text of every WIN tagged code_analysis.post_hoc=true, EXCEPT WINs already containing prospective/timestamp language. Decider applies as individual wins.json patches.
author: analyst
kernel_of_truth: The dome author is not lying about his workflow: he really does timestamp prediction files before running fetch scripts, and in the few cases where the expected value is not already known (see Mode 4 globe-fingerprint tracker), the workflow could in principle produce a real prospective test. Our criticism is structural: the workflow only produces a prospective test when the expected value is NOT hardcoded in the same commit as the fetch, AND the value is not already public. We find zero WINs in V51.1 where both conditions hold, but we leave the door open via the legitimate-novelty carve-out.
methodology: Read all 68 WINs tagged code_analysis.post_hoc=true in wins.json. Classified each into T (textbook common-knowledge), D (field-specific published dataset), V (dome-derived/speculative), or O (obscure, flagged for verdict review). Checked existing detail text for presence of author-definition language (strings: prospective, timestamp, pre-regist, before confirming). 6 WINs already address the author definition and are excluded from patching: 014, 025, 037, 058b, 061, 065. The remaining 62 WINs receive one of four standardized append sentences keyed to their category. Obscure cases (O) receive a verdict-review flag rather than a final rebuttal.

append_sentence_templates.T: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script "predicted" it before reading the data. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable. No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies: the value is not obscure and the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. See Part 3.1b.
append_sentence_templates.D: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
append_sentence_templates.V: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters (disc radius, firmament height, chosen symmetry axis, or similar), and the "confirmation" consists of matching that derivation to a globe-measured value — which, because the dome parameters were themselves fit to globe data (see Kill-Shot #1 and Part 2b), is circular. There is no pre-registration of the derivation in a venue that timestamps independently of the author, and the dome monitor script (where one exists) encodes the expected value in the same commit as the comparison. Under the dome author's own definition and our stricter standard in Part 3.1b, this is retrodiction, not prediction.
append_sentence_templates.O: FLAGGED FOR VERDICT REVIEW. This WIN uses a value that is plausibly obscure (non-indexed archival measurement, non-standard source, or otherwise not common textbook knowledge). Under the legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b, an obscure value combined with a unique dome-framework derivation that does not reduce to a textbook formula could in principle qualify as a legitimate independent rediscovery. The analyst has not been able to verify (a) that the source is in fact unknown-to-author at write-time, or (b) that the derivation is genuinely unique to the dome framework. Until both are established, the post_hoc tag stands and the rebuttal sentence above applies, but the verdict may warrant a targeted re-review once the provenance is established.

obscure_flagged_for_verdict_review.note: Manually verify: (a) is the confirming value actually obscure (non-indexed, non-textbook), (b) is the dome derivation unique to its framework and not algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. If both yes, consider retagging post_hoc=false and invoking legitimate-novelty carve-out.

Manually verify: (a) is the confirming value actually obscure (non-indexed, non-textbook), (b) is the dome derivation unique to its framework and not algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. If both yes, consider retagging post_hoc=false and invoking legitimate-novelty carve-out.

Manually verify: (a) is the confirming value actually obscure (non-indexed, non-textbook), (b) is the dome derivation unique to its framework and not algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. If both yes, consider retagging post_hoc=false and invoking legitimate-novelty carve-out.

cross_reference_iss_677: The prospective wins pipeline (Mode 5, not yet implemented) must apply the SAME four-category classification and the SAME carve-out test as this audit. Specifically: a claim listed as "prospective" on the dome site can only be accepted as prospective by our review if it fails the trivial-transcription test of Part 3.1b AND the derivation is unique to the dome framework AND the expected value is not hardcoded in the same commit as the fetch call. Audit template: for each PROS-NNN item, run the same T/D/V/O classification and require an affirmative novelty finding before even considering the WIN as "open".
decider_integration_note: For each row in per_win_assignments with already_addressed=false, append the matching category template to the end of that WIN's detail_verdict_text field in wins.json (NOT detail_evidence). Patches should be grouped by category to keep patch files small. After integration, the curmudgeon priority queue should receive a review request so the new per-WIN language is sanity-checked before V6.2. Obscure (O) cases should be integrated as FLAGS only — do not commit a final append sentence; instead, write the FLAG text verbatim into detail_verdict_text and create a new issue for manual verdict review.
holes_addressed: ISS-697: no per-WIN rebuttal tied to author definition
Curmudgeon Cycle-1 finding: our post_hoc tag lacks a visible per-WIN rationale
Cross-consistency: all 62 non-already-addressed WINs now share one of four exact templates, eliminating language drift across the review
new_evidence_added: Explicit list of 4 obscure cases flagged for verdict review (003, 011, 015, plus any future additions)

anticipated_objections.objection: You said you would check dome git history for genuine pre-registration (commits where expected value was added BEFORE the fetch call). You did not.
anticipated_objections.response: Correct. This first-pass audit does not include the per-commit git-history check. That check is deferred to a follow-on expansion (proposed: EXP-054b) because it requires cloning the dome repository and running a per-WIN script against its commit log. The rebuttal sentences are written so that if the git-history check later finds a clean pre-registration for a specific WIN, that WIN can be upgraded without invalidating the other 61. In the absence of the check, we rely on the structural smoking-gun argument from Part 3.1b: the monitor.py/pull_data.py pattern of hardcoded-expected-in-same-commit-as-fetch, which we have already audited for the hardcoded-monitoring majority.

anticipated_objections.objection: Lumping 68 WINs into four categories with four template sentences is lazy.
anticipated_objections.response: The templates are intentionally standardized because the failure mode is structural and repeats across WINs. Writing 62 bespoke paragraphs would introduce exactly the language drift that Cycle-1 flagged. The template sentences are designed to be read in the context of each WIN's existing detail_evidence — which already contains the WIN-specific scientific rebuttal. This audit adds only the universal meta-critique (author definition + structural post_hoc finding) on top of that.

anticipated_objections.objection: You might be wrong about "textbook" classifications. Schumann 7.83 Hz is textbook, but the dome site claims its predicted value from geometry.
anticipated_objections.response: The kernel of truth we acknowledge is that the dome site does offer a geometric derivation (H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) → fundamental cavity mode). Our Self-Contradicted verdict on WIN-038 notes the derivation yields ~22 Hz, not 7.83 Hz — so the dome is both failing its own geometry AND hardcoding the textbook 7.83 Hz as the expected value. The category T tag is correct precisely because the value in the monitoring script is 7.83 Hz (textbook), not ~22 Hz (what the geometry actually predicts).
